slides.title: Python's Yes/No Answer
slides.body: Every program needs to make decisions. Python uses **booleans** тАФ values that are either `True` or `False` тАФ to represent those yes/no answers.
slides.tip: Booleans are named after mathematician George Boole, who formalized logic with just two states.

slides.title: Capital Letters Are Required
slides.body: Write `True` and `False` with a **capital first letter** тАФ always. Python is case-sensitive, so `true` or `false` (lowercase) will throw a `NameError`.
slides.tip: bool is its own type in Python тАФ just like int or str.

slides.title: Comparisons Make Booleans
slides.body: You rarely type `True` yourself. Instead, write a **comparison** and Python gives you the boolean result. Use `==`, `!=`, `>`, `<`, `>=`, or `<=`.
slides.tip: `=` stores a value; `==` asks "are these equal?" тАФ very different things!

slides.title: Truthy, Falsy, and None
slides.body: Every Python value secretly acts like a boolean. **Falsy** values (`0`, `""`, `[]`, `None`) behave like `False`; everything else is **truthy**. `None` is Python's way of saying "nothing here at all".
slides.tip: Instead of `if len(my_list) > 0:`, just write `if my_list:` тАФ Python does the check for you.

slides.title: Check None With `is`
slides.body: To test whether a variable is `None`, use `is None` тАФ not `== None`. It's the Python convention and is more precise.
slides.tip: There is only one `None` in Python, so `is` is the exact right check.

slides.title: You've Got Booleans Figured Out
slides.body: You now know that `True`/`False` represent yes/no decisions, comparisons produce booleans automatically, every value is truthy or falsy, and `None` signals the absence of a value.
slides.tip: Bonus fact: `True` is secretly `1` and `False` is `0` тАФ try `True + True` in the REPL!
